pattern: Keeping the fewest elements to reach a non-decreasing order is the same question as keeping the most elements that already form one — so the answer is the array's length minus its longest non-decreasing subsequence, and with only three possible values that longest run can be tracked with three running counters instead of a full subsequence table.
hints: The elements you remove are exactly the ones NOT part of some longest already-non-decreasing subsequence, so minimize removals by maximizing the length of such a subsequence.

Because every value is 1, 2, or 3, track three running best-lengths as you scan left to right: the best run ending in a 1, the best run ending in a 2 (which may extend a run ending in 1 or 2), and the best run ending in a 3 (which may extend a run ending in 1, 2, or 3).
After the scan, the longest non-decreasing subsequence is the largest of the three running counters, and the answer is the array length minus that value.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, several possible optimal removals
statement example, one clean optimal removal
strictly decreasing three-element run, must strip down to one
a repeating 1-2-3 pattern where the longest run interleaves across repetitions
a 3 stuck in front of an otherwise-sorted run, only removing the leading value helps

stress.description: random arrays of 1/2/3 across the full allowed length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 3) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
